Wren pushes the old rig too hard, chasing range it was really never built to give her. And it at last dies in a thin wisp of blue smoke and the sharp, acrid smell of scorched solder. The attic goes fully quiet all at once, the hum simply gone. Her grandfather's transmitter gives out on the very same night Alder's loop does, some cruel coincidence she can't quite process yet. And Wren is left sitting there in the dark with a ruined set and a message she never really managed to send.

Wren rings the amateur radio club's emergency line, her fingers shaking as she dials the number from memory. A sleepy operator answers on the third ring, half annoyed at first. Then he wakes fully as her whole tangled story spills out in one desperate rush. He could throw Alder's message onto the county-wide net right away, reaching dozens of stations at once for the widest possible chance somebody hears it. Or, if she is simply unlucky with the timing tonight, she might learn instead that nobody is really on shift right now. And only an after-hours recorder is listening at this particular hour.

choices.label: Push it onto the county net now.
Reach only the after-hours recorder.

nodes.body: The club fires Alder's message across the whole county net at once. Dozens of stations relay Marin's name out into the storm, one after another in quick succession. Within minutes, reports start trickling back through the crackling static. Wren has to judge, fast, whether the strongest reply is the lead she has been praying for all night. It comes from a town two ridges west, where a woman is, it seems, asking urgent, oddly specific questions. Or maybe it is simply a coincidence, not worth chasing this close to the very end of the clock.

choices.label: Chase the lead two ridges west.
Log every lead for the club to follow.

nodes.body: The lead turns out to be real. The woman two ridges west is Marin herself, up late and idly scanning bands she abandoned decades ago out of old habit more than any real hope. Hearing her own name ride in suddenly on her father's old loop stops her cold, phone halfway to her ear before she even decides to answer it. The club patches her straight through to the relay without delay, and Kell Alder's thirty-year wait ends at last, in one single trembling hello.

Wren cannot be sure any single lead tonight is really Marin. The clock is too close to running out for that. So she logs every one carefully anyway, and hands the club a complete file: the signal number, the whole story, every promising callback she managed to gather. The net keeps the message circulating night after night, from here on out. Wren herself never gets to see how it all ends, but Alder's loop is finally sitting safely in a hundred searching hands, instead of just one tired old man's.

Only the after-hours recorder ever answers her call, and Wren leaves the whole story into that empty electronic void anyway. She hopes desperately that somebody plays it back first thing at dawn tomorrow. Dawn, though, comes well after the countdown does. By the time a club volunteer really listens to her recorded message the next morning, the relay loop has already fallen silent for good out on the headland. Her call arrives just one long, rain-soaked night too late to matter at all.

Wren stands in the rain with her phone in her hand. She cannot do this alone against a clock this unforgiving, not tonight. She could phone the coast guard auxiliary instead. They treat any real beacon on the headland seriously. And they can reach the relay by road in minutes if she convinces them fast enough. Or she could track down one of Alder's old colleagues from the relay's working days. That person would understand every word of what she found tonight, without her needing to explain any of it twice.

choices.label: Call the coast guard auxiliary.
Find Alder old colleague.

nodes.body: The auxiliary duty officer listens closely the moment Wren speaks. A beacon on the headland is counting down, she tells him, right in the middle of a storm. He could believe her at once and dispatch a crew to the relay to secure it as a live signal. Or he could hesitate instead. A mystery loop is a hard thing to log as a real emergency. All he has to go on, after all, is one schoolgirl's word over the phone.

choices.label: Convince him to dispatch a crew.
Watch him dismiss it as static.

nodes.body: A crew rolls hard for the headland. Wren guides them by phone from her kitchen table, a map spread flat under the lamp. They reach the relay well ahead of the worst of the storm. Over the radio, they tell her the console inside is alive, still counting down steadily, just as she described. Wren has to tell them, right now, what matters most in this last remaining minute. Should they trace the message toward its intended listener above everything else? Or should they focus fully on saving the failing station itself, whole and intact, for whoever comes looking later on?

choices.label: Have them trace and reach Marin.
Have them secure and save the relay.

nodes.body: The crew patches Wren straight into the relay's own system from the road. Their equipment backs up Alder's fragile thirty-year loop. And the signal leaps well past its old, tired limits at last. Within the hour they trace the return call to Marin. They hold the channel open steadily the whole time. At long last, across all that accumulated silence, father and daughter are really speaking to each other.

The crew secures the relay only minutes before the worst of the storm hits. They swap out the dead power cell and lock the loop safely onto backup power. Nothing at all is lost. With the station at last stable, the auxiliary opens a real search for Marin the very next morning. They credit the whole discovery to the one determined girl who would not let a strange signal go unanswered.

The officer has no real emergency to log. So he thanks Wren politely and files her call under curiosities. No crew ever rolls out that night. Alone on the headland, the countdown runs down with no one watching. The loop at last dies somewhere in the gale. The one report that might have saved it sits closed inside a duty logbook that nobody opens again.

Wren tracks down Alder's old relay colleague, a retired man named Ruiz who lives quietly across the bay. She finds his number buried deep in her grandfather's old papers. He goes fully quiet on the phone the moment she describes the loop in careful detail. Then he admits softly, almost to himself, that he always feared Kell would later do just this. He could drive out right now to help her reach the station in time, his keys and his know-how both still intact after all these years. Or she might learn instead that his number has been dead for years, the man himself long since moved away without leaving a single trace behind him.

choices.label: Team up with Ruiz to reach the relay.
Find the colleague long gone.

nodes.body: Ruiz meets Wren right at the headland gate, jangling a heavy set of keys he never quite got around to returning after all these years away. He knows this whole console cold, far better than Wren ever could manage alone tonight. Together, they could complete Alder's scheduled transmission the right way, doing it just right before the power cell at last dies for good on its own. Or Wren could simply step back now instead and let the old professional take over fully from here. Her own part in this whole long search would already be finished, either way, tonight.

choices.label: Complete the transmission together.
Step back and let Ruiz finish it.

nodes.body: Side by side, Wren and Ruiz bring the relay up to full power together at last. They send Alder's message out clean and strong, on the exact frequency Marin once knew by heart, years ago. When the answer at last comes back, faint at first and then clearly sure, Ruiz grips Wren's shoulder hard, without a single word needed between them. The loop that counted down all alone for thirty straight years ends, at last, with two people really there together to hear it land.

Wren hands Ruiz the whole tangled story and steps back quietly. And he takes over at once with the easy, practiced confidence of a man who helped build this exact place with his own two hands decades ago. She bikes home certain it's really in good hands now, more relieved than disappointed by the whole thing. Yet she never really hears how it all ends, whether Marin answered at last or the loop simply, at last stopped fully on its own out there. Her long, strange night closes on a question she suspects she will carry with her for a very long time to come.

The number rings out to a confused stranger. He has never heard of Ruiz, or Alder, or any relay station at all. Ruiz moved away years ago, it turns out. And he left no forwarding line behind him. Every other name from the relay's old working days has either scattered across the country or passed on fully by now. Wren sits there with the dead phone still warm in her hand. The clock keeps running down regardless of anything she does. The one person who might have really helped her is already gone, well beyond any reach she has left tonight.
